source.subject: Request: encryption controls evidence and key provenance for SIG/CAIQ review (Acme Payments)
messages:
From: Alicia Gomez <alicia.gomez@secureassessors.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>, Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: compliance@acme-payments.com
Date: 2026-11-16T08:12:00-08:00
Subject: Request: encryption controls evidence and key provenance for SIG/CAIQ review (Acme Payments)

Hi Ben / Monica,

Acme's compliance team has kicked off the SIG/CAIQ pass for our upcoming vendor assessment. They asked us to collect artefacts around encryption and key management. Could Redwood provide the following items by EOD Thu (PST)? A single consolidated package would be preferred.

Requested items (high level):
- Artifacts showing key lifecycle and rotation schedules for customer-data keys (any exported rotation logs are fine)
- HSM attestation or vendor statement for root key material / KM integration
- KMS access matrix showing roles and who can perform unwrap/rotate/import operations
- A short narrative (1 page) on in-transit vs at-rest controls and where keys are stored (BYOK notes if applicable)

Additionally, please note any known exceptions or tickets where key material required manual handling. We may follow up with specific SIG questions.

Friendly thanks — we know this is a common ask. If you can confirm an ETA and whether some of these items are internal-only (i.e., redaction required), that helps our reviewers.

Regards,
Alicia Gomez
Senior Assessor, SecureAssessors
A: audits@secureassessors.com

---
Alicia G.
On behalf of Acme Payments compliance team

From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
To: Alicia Gomez <alicia.gomez@secureassessors.com>
Cc: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwoodinference.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwoodinference.com>, Vivek Kulkarni <vivek.kulkarni@redwoodinference.com>
Date: 2026-11-16T09:05:00-08:00
Subject: Re: Request: encryption controls evidence and key provenance for SIG/CAIQ review (Acme Payments)

Thanks Alicia — we can assemble a consolidated packet.

Quick notes / plan:
- Marcus (crypto ops) will pull rotation logs and the access matrix.
- Vivek (security) will attach the latest HSM attestation and confirm any exportable statements.
- Monica will review for redactions and confirm what we can share with external auditors.

Target ETA: send full package and a short playbook by 2026-11-18 5pm PST. If anything stalls, we'll flag it by EOD 11/17.

Ben Carter
Senior Sales Engineer | Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwoodinference.com

From: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>, Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Vivek Kulkarni <vivek.kulkarni@redwoodinference.com>
Date: 2026-11-17T10:23:00-08:00
Subject: Re: Request: encryption controls evidence and key provenance for SIG/CAIQ review (Acme Payments)

Ben / Monica — attaching the artifacts I pulled from crypto-ops: rotation_schedule_2026.csv (redacted internal identifiers), kms_access_matrix_2026.xlsx, and a short notes file describing rotation triggers.

Summary of contents:
- rotation_schedule_2026.csv: canonical schedule for master and customer data keys (monthly automated for ephemeral keys; annual root rotation with recorded maintenance windows).
- kms_access_matrix_2026.xlsx: RBAC matrix showing which Redwood roles (ops, platform-auth, se) have key management permissions; columns indicate key unwrap, rotate, import, and emergency procedures.
- encryption_key_lifecycle_playbook.md: narrative covering generation, wrapping, storage, and decommissioning steps; includes references to our KMS provider and HSM vendor.

Notes:
- HSM root material is under vendor custody; we have an attestation from the vendor (requesting Vivek to drop the file from security).
- No customer symmetric key material is stored in plaintext on our platform; all customer-derived keys are wrapped under a KMS-managed envelope key.

If you want, I can redact ticket IDs before external delivery — flagged sensitive entries in the CSV with <REDACTED>.

Marcus Lin
Crypto Operations Lead | Redwood Inference
marcus.lin@redwoodinference.com

From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
To: Alicia Gomez <alicia.gomez@secureassessors.com>
Cc: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwoodinference.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwoodinference.com>, Vivek Kulkarni <vivek.kulkarni@redwoodinference.com>
Date: 2026-11-18T13:01:00-08:00
Subject: Re: Request: encryption controls evidence and key provenance for SIG/CAIQ review (Acme Payments)

Alicia — attaching the consolidated packet per your list. Files are included as attachments and there is a read-only Drive folder for larger artifacts: https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1A2b3C-encryption-evidence

Attachments (linked / attached):
- rotation_schedule_2026.csv (redacted ticket ids)
- kms_access_matrix_2026.xlsx
- hsm_attestation_q3_2026.pdf (vendor-signed attestation; redacted assertion numbers)
- encryption_key_lifecycle_playbook.md (1.2 pages)

Short answers to common SIG items:
- At-rest: customer data is encrypted with per-customer envelope keys; envelope keys are rotated per schedule and wrapped by an HSM-backed KMS.
- In-transit: TLS 1.2+ with modern ciphers; internal RPCs use mTLS and are permitted to use short-lived session keys.
- Key rotation: automated monthly for ephemeral keys, on-demand for compromised keys, and annual scheduled rotation for root/keystore pairs.

If you need an attestation letter on company letterhead for the HSM statement, Vivek can provide that — suggest a 30-minute call tomorrow to walk through the artifacts if reviewers want context.

Ben Carter
Senior Sales Engineer | Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwoodinference.com
Attachments: rotation_schedule_2026.csv (text/csv), kms_access_matrix_2026.xlsx (application/vnd.openxmlformats-officedocument.spreadsheetml.sheet), hsm_attestation_q3_2026.pdf (application/pdf), encryption_key_lifecycle_playbook.md (text/markdown)

From: Alicia Gomez <alicia.gomez@secureassessors.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: compliance@acme-payments.com
Date: 2026-11-18T14:03:00-08:00
Subject: Re: Request: encryption controls evidence and key provenance for SIG/CAIQ review (Acme Payments)

Thanks Ben — received the packet and the Drive link. Quick clarifications from Acme reviewers:
1) Can you confirm whether customer-provided keys (BYOK) are supported and, if so, whether the envelope key wrapping happens in the HSM or in the KMS software layer?
2) For the rotation_schedule_2026.csv row labeled "root rotation", can you point to the ticket or change window record (we need the maintenance window ID for traceability)? If the ticket ID is sensitive, a cross-reference to HBSP-4821 in the related links is fine.

If you can respond by EOD today, we can close this portion of the SIG. Otherwise, we may request a short call with Vivek to get the attestation letter on file.

Appreciate the fast turnaround.

Best,
Alicia
Senior Assessor, SecureAssessors

source.subject: Allocation discrepancy: Novacloud payment applied to wrong invoice (84321) - journal and mapping
messages:
From: NovaCloud Payments <payments@novacloud.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-11-15T09:12:00-08:00
Subject: Payment allocation for invoice 84321 (Novacloud)

Hi Ben,

We processed a customer remittance today for $42,500 that our system applied to invoice 84322 (subscription renewal) instead of 84321 (one-time integration fee). Attached is the payment manifest and transmittal (novacloud_payment_manifest.xlsx). Can you confirm Redwood's AR position for 84321 and advise whether you want us to reapply the payment to 84321 or if you will post an internal reallocation?

There is also a VAT split on that remittance (20% EU VAT on the one-time fee). Our team needs confirmation on whether VAT should be moved between GL accounts or handled via a corrective journal.

Thanks,
Omar Fernandez
Payments Operations — NovaCloud

Attachment: novacloud_payment_manifest.xlsx (spreadsheet, 72KB)

From: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>
To: Laura Bennett <laura_bennett@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.com>
Cc: NovaCloud Payments <payments@novacloud.com>
Date: 2026-11-15T10:03:00-08:00
Subject: Re: Payment allocation for invoice 84321 (Novacloud)

Thanks Omar — forwarding to Finance. Laura / Marcus, Nova says $42,500 was applied incorrectly to 84322 instead of 84321. The one-time integration fee (84321) includes EU VAT at 20% that must be reflected in our VAT control account. Nova is open to reapplying on their side but asked whether we prefer them to or if we will post a reallocation journal.

Requesting:
- Confirmation of AR balances for 84321/84322
- Proposed GL entries (reversal + reapply or direct allocation) and whether to use 2210-Suspense temporary account
- Any revenue-recognition mapping impact (should this move between REC-030 one-time vs REC-100 subscription buckets)

I attached a quick extract from our collections view (payment_report_20261115.csv).

Ben
Senior AE, Enterprise Sales
Redwood Inference

Attachment: payment_report_20261115.csv (CSV, 18KB)

From: Laura Bennett <laura_bennett@redwood.com>
To: Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-11-16T08:22:00-08:00
Subject: Re: Payment allocation for invoice 84321 (Novacloud)

Ben — thanks for flagging. Marcus, can you please evaluate and prepare a draft journal? Notes/guidance below: 

- Current AR: invoice 84321 (one-time) shows as open for $35,000 net + $7,000 VAT (20%); invoice 84322 (subscription) was fully paid, but remittance shows an extra $42,500 applied.
- Preferred approach: reverse the erroneous application on 84322 back to 2210-Suspense, then reapply to 84321, posting VAT reallocation to VAT-PAYABLE (GL 2430).
- Revenue mapping: confirm whether the $35k net portion should remain REC-030 (professional services) — if the customer was already revenue-recognized under subscription timing, we may need a revenue-recognition correction. Check with RevRec team if recognition date changes.

I drafted a suggested GL mapping in gl_mapping_suggestion.csv (attached) — uses JV flow: reverse (Date 2026-11-16) -> suspense -> reapply -> VAT corrective. Marcus, please prepare JV-<id> draft and note any RevRec impact; copy Mina for sign-off if the net > $25k.

Laura Bennett
Corporate Accounting Manager
Redwood Inference

Attachment: gl_mapping_suggestion.csv (CSV, 4KB)

From: Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.com>
To: Laura Bennett <laura_bennett@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>
Cc: NovaCloud Payments <payments@novacloud.com>
Date: 2026-11-16T11:45:00-08:00
Subject: Re: Payment allocation for invoice 84321 (Novacloud)

On Mon, Nov 16, 2026 at 08:22, Laura Bennett <laura_bennett@redwood.com> wrote:
> Marcus, can you please evaluate and prepare a draft journal?

Will do. Plan:
1) Post reversal of $42,500 from 4010-AR (invoice 84322) to 2210-Suspense on 2026-11-16 (JV draft attached).
2) Re-allocate $42,500 from 2210-Suspense to 4010-AR (invoice 84321) and post VAT corrective to 2430-VAT-PAYABLE.
3) Tag JV with reference SUP-3721 and include Nova's remittance as support.

Potential RevRec impact: if REC-030 (one-time) was already recognized per customer acceptance, nothing further; if subscription revenue was recognized on 84322 that included the $35k, we will need a revenue shift journal to move between REC-100 and REC-030. I'll confirm after a quick RevRec check — should have this by EOD.

Draft JV attached: JV-2026-11-16-draft.pdf.

Marcus Lin
Senior Accountant - AR
Redwood Inference

Attachment: JV-2026-11-16-draft.pdf (PDF, 36KB)

From: Mina Haddad <mina_haddad@redwood.com>
To: Laura Bennett <laura_bennett@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-11-17T09:05:00-08:00
Subject: Approval: JV and reallocation for NovaCloud remittance

Reviewed the draft JV (JV-2026-11-16-draft.pdf) and gl mapping. Approve the reversal -> suspense -> reapply flow. Conditions:
- Use 2210-Suspense only as an intra-day holding account; clear within 48 hours of posting.
- Log RevRec decision on the JV description; if a revenue-recognition shift is required, tag as REVREC-ADJ and include cross-reference to RevRec ticket (create if needed).
- Notify NovaCloud when posting is complete and send an updated remittance confirmation.

Marcus: proceed to post today and cc me on the confirmation to Nova.

Mina Haddad
Director, Finance Operations
Redwood Inference

From: NovaCloud Payments <payments@novacloud.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-11-17T16:45:00-08:00
Subject: Re: Payment allocation for invoice 84321 (Novacloud)

Thanks — we confirm reapplication on our side is fine if you prefer us to do it, but we will hold until you post the JV to ensure AR totals align. Please send confirmation once the JV is posted and VAT corrective is recorded so we can update our ledgers.

Regards,
Omar Fernandez
Payments Operations — NovaCloud

Attached is the payment manifest and transmittal (novacloud_payment_manifest.xlsx). Can you confirm Redwood's AR position for 84321 and advise whether you want us to reapply the payment to 84321 or if you will post an internal reallocation?

There is also a VAT split on that remittance (20% EU VAT on the one-time fee). Our team needs confirmation on whether VAT should be moved between GL accounts or handled via a corrective journal.

Thanks,
Omar Fernandez
Payments Operations — NovaCloud

To: Laura Bennett <laura_bennett@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.com>
Cc: NovaCloud Payments <payments@novacloud.com>
Date: 2026-11-15T10:03:00-08:00
Subject: Re: Payment allocation for invoice 84321 (Novacloud)

Thanks Omar — forwarding to Finance. Laura / Marcus, Nova says $42,500 was applied incorrectly to 84322 instead of 84321. The one-time integration fee (84321) includes EU VAT at 20% that must be reflected in our VAT control account. Nova is open to reapplying on their side but asked whether we prefer them to or if we will post a reallocation journal.

Requesting:
- Confirmation of AR balances for 84321/84322
- Proposed GL entries (reversal + reapply or direct allocation) and whether to use 2210-Suspense temporary account
- Any revenue-recognition mapping impact (should this move between REC-030 one-time vs REC-100 subscription buckets)

Ben

From: Laura Bennett <laura_bennett@redwood.com>
To: Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-11-16T08:22:00-08:00
Subject: Re: Payment allocation for invoice 84321 (Novacloud)

Ben — thanks for flagging. Marcus, can you please evaluate and prepare a draft journal? Notes/guidance below: 

- Current AR: invoice 84321 (one-time) shows as open for $35,000 net + $7,000 VAT (20%); invoice 84322 (subscription) was fully paid, but remittance shows an extra $42,500 applied.
- Preferred approach: reverse the erroneous application on 84322 back to 2210-Suspense, then reapply to 84321, posting VAT reallocation to VAT-PAYABLE (GL 2430).
- Revenue mapping: confirm whether the $35k net portion should remain REC-030 (professional services) — if the customer was already revenue-recognized under subscription timing, we may need a revenue-recognition correction. Check with RevRec team if recognition date changes.

I drafted a suggested GL mapping in gl_mapping_suggestion.csv (attached) — uses JV flow: reverse (Date 2026-11-16) -> suspense -> reapply -> VAT corrective. Marcus, please prepare JV-<id> draft and note any RevRec impact; copy Mina for sign-off if the net > $25k.

Laura Bennett
Corporate Accounting Manager
Redwood Inference

Attachment: gl_mapping_suggestion.csv (CSV, 4KB)

From: Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.com>

Cc: NovaCloud Payments <payments@novacloud.com>

Subject: Re: Payment allocation for invoice 84321 (Novacloud)

On Mon, Nov 16, 2026 at 08:22, Laura Bennett <laura_bennett@redwood.com> wrote:
> Marcus, can you please evaluate and prepare a draft journal?

Will do. Plan:
1) Post reversal of $42,500 from 4010-AR (invoice 84322) to 2210-Suspense on 2026-11-16 (JV draft attached).
2) Re-allocate $42,500 from 2210-Suspense to 4010-AR (invoice 84321) and post VAT corrective to 2430-VAT-PAYABLE.
3) Tag JV with reference SUP-3721 and include Nova's remittance as support.

Potential RevRec impact: if REC-030 (one-time) was already recognized per customer acceptance, nothing further; if subscription revenue was recognized on 84322 that included the $35k, we will need a revenue shift journal to move between REC-100 and REC-030. I'll confirm after a quick RevRec check — should have this by EOD.

Draft JV attached: JV-2026-11-16-draft.pdf.

Marcus Lin
Senior Accountant - AR
Redwood Inference

Attachment: JV-2026-11-16-draft.pdf (PDF, 36KB)

From: Mina Haddad <mina_haddad@redwood.com>
To: Laura Bennett <laura_bennett@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-11-17T09:05:00-08:00
Subject: Approval: JV and reallocation for NovaCloud remittance

Reviewed the draft JV (JV-2026-11-16-draft.pdf) and gl mapping. Approve the reversal -> suspense -> reapply flow. Conditions:
- Use 2210-Suspense only as an intra-day holding account; clear within 48 hours of posting.
- Log RevRec decision on the JV description; if a revenue-recognition shift is required, tag as REVREC-ADJ and include cross-reference to RevRec ticket (create if needed).
- Notify NovaCloud when posting is complete and send an updated remittance confirmation.

Marcus: proceed to post today and cc me on the confirmation to Nova.

Mina Haddad
Director, Finance Operations
Redwood Inference

From: NovaCloud Payments <payments@novacloud.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-11-17T16:45:00-08:00
Subject: Re: Payment allocation for invoice 84321 (Novacloud)

Thanks — we confirm reapplication on our side is fine if you prefer us to do it, but we will hold until you post the JV to ensure AR totals align. Please send confirmation once the JV is posted and VAT corrective is recorded so we can update our ledgers.

Regards,
Omar Fernandez
Payments Operations — NovaCloud

source.subject: Silverline AI — silverline3b: intermittent 5xx/timeouts (investigation & recovery)
messages:
From: Priya Rao <priya.rao@silverline.ai>
To: Aisha Rahman <aisha_rahman@redwood.com>
Cc: Ethan Cole <ethan.cole@silverline.ai>
Date: Mon, 16 Nov 2026 08:57:00 -0800
Subject: [ACTION REQUEST] silverline3b — intermittent 5xx/timeouts impacting batch inference

Hi Aisha,

We started seeing elevated 5xx and timeout errors from silverline3b around 08:30 PST this morning. The failures are most common on long context requests (8k+ tokens) for our batch scoring job — roughly 12% of calls are returning 5xx or hanging until timeout.

Impact: the nightly scoring job failed to finish; downstream A/B scoring is delayed. Can Redwood confirm whether anything changed on your side? We don't have any configuration changes on our end.

I've attached a short sample trace and a timeline CSV export. Please let us know next steps and whether we should divert traffic to an alternate model variant. We're happy to schedule a quick bridge.

Thanks,
Priya Rao
Head of ML Ops
Silverline AI

Attachments: silverline-sample-trace-20261116.log, silverline-timeline-20261116.csv
From: Aisha Rahman <aisha_rahman@redwood.com>
To: Priya Rao <priya.rao@silverline.ai>, Ethan Cole <ethan.cole@silverline.ai>
Cc: Rishi Malhotra <rishi_malhotra@redwood.com>, Monica Patel <monica_patel@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 16 Nov 2026 09:12:00 -0800
Subject: Re: [ACTION REQUEST] silverline3b — intermittent 5xx/timeouts impacting batch inference

Hi Priya — thanks for the quick heads up and for the attachments. We see the same error spikes on our side and have opened a high-priority triage (INF-4521).

Immediate actions we've taken:
- Routed your production route to the silverline3b-fallback variant (same weights) to reduce exposure to the affected shard.
- Throttled concurrent batch window for requests >6k tokens to reduce peak KV pressure.
- Ops is collecting debug dumps; can you also forward any additional traces for 5xx occurrences (X-Request-Id header values are helpful).

Rishi (SRE) and Monica (CS) are on the thread — Rishi is leading the technical investigation and we'll provide a timeline update within 3 hours. Apologies for the disruption; we'll also follow up with a full post-incident summary and remediation plan.

Best,
Aisha Rahman
Account Executive | Redwood Inference

Note: Temporary routing change in place — we will not change your billed model variant without sign-off.
From: Rishi Malhotra <rishi_malhotra@redwood.com>
To: Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.com>, Monica Patel <monica_patel@redwood.com>
Cc: Aisha Rahman <aisha_rahman@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 16 Nov 2026 10:05:00 -0800
Subject: [INTERNAL] initial findings: silverline3b 5xx spike (KV cache eviction)

Marcus / Monica — quick sync notes from the triage:
- Symptom: sudden increase in 5xxs originating in the model-serving process when handling requests with context >8k tokens. Error path shows failed async disk spills from the KV cache layer followed by an unhandled exception in the request worker.
- Root cause (interim): an eviction storm on per-model KV shards caused by a corner-case in the LRU eviction where async spill tasks were delayed under IO pressure; when the spill failed the worker would surface a 5xx instead of gracefully falling back to in-memory degrade path.
- Evidence: attached kv-eviction-dump (sample of shard stats) and server traces correlate the spike with multiple parallel batch windows starting at ~08:28 PST.

Mitigations applied (10:00 PST):
1) Hotfix deployed to worker process to catch spill failures and route to the in-memory fallback (kv-spill-safety v1).
2) Increased KV in-memory threshold for silverline3b to reduce spill frequency for the next 48h.
3) Backpressure rule added: concurrent long-context batches for the route limited to 6 at once.

Next steps:
- Full patch to adjust eviction policy and improve async retry semantics (Marcus to own).
- Canary the fix into silverline3b-fallback tomorrow morning (small %).
- Postmortem to include monitoring hooks for pending async spill queue length and failed-spill-rate alarm.

I'll add timing notes to INF-4521 and attach the debug artifacts.

Rishi Malhotra
Principal SRE, Redwood Inference

Attachments: kv-eviction-dump-20261116.json.zip
From: Aisha Rahman <aisha_rahman@redwood.com>
To: Priya Rao <priya.rao@silverline.ai>, Ethan Cole <ethan.cole@silverline.ai>
Cc: Rishi Malhotra <rishi_malhotra@redwood.com>, Monica Patel <monica_patel@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 16 Nov 2026 16:42:00 -0800
Subject: silverline3b update: mitigation steps completed and next steps

Hi Priya / Ethan —

Quick update now that our mitigations have been applied and validated across your routes:

What happened (short):
We observed an eviction cascade in the per-model KV cache that, under sustained long-context batch traffic, triggered async spill failures which were not being handled by the worker. That failure path produced elevated 5xx/timeouts for requests that needed KV spill I/O.

What we did for you (completed):
- Turned on kv-spill-safety v1 which intercepts spill failures and returns the in-memory degrade path instead of failing the request; deployed at 10:00 PST.
- Increased the in-memory threshold for silverline3b for 48 hours to avoid immediate spills while we finish the fix.
- Temporarily routed your traffic to the fallback variant and added short-term concurrency limits for long-context batches.

Result: Error rate for your account fell from ~12% to <0.8% across the afternoon sampling window. You should see normal job completion for your nightly scoring run; Monica will validate job completion with your team.

Planned longer-term fixes (timeline):
- Eviction policy change + improved async spill retry semantics (engineering patch) — target: 2026-11-19 canary deploy.
- New alarms for failed-spill-rate and pending-spill-queue (in monitoring) — target: 2026-11-18.
- Postmortem and RCA doc to be shared by 2026-11-20 with concrete remediation and compensating controls.

Compensation: We will apply a credit for the impacted time window against November usage; our billing team will follow up once the window is finalized.

Action items for Silverline AI:
- If you see any remaining 5xxs, please forward the X-Request-Id values or the trace attachments to support@redwood.com so we can correlate.
- If you’d like a short bridge tomorrow for Marcus to walk through the technical fix, let me know preferred times.

Thanks for your patience — we know this disrupted an important job and we appreciate the quick signal from your team.

Best,
Aisha Rahman
Account Executive | Redwood Inference
Direct: (415) 555-0193

---
Aisha Rahman
Redwood Inference — Customer-facing team
From: Priya Rao <priya.rao@silverline.ai>
To: Aisha Rahman <aisha_rahman@redwood.com>
Cc: Ethan Cole <ethan.cole@silverline.ai>, Monica Patel <monica_patel@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 17 Nov 2026 09:03:00 -0800
Subject: Re: silverline3b update: mitigation steps completed and next steps

Thanks — confirming error rate dropped and our nightly job completed this morning. Appreciate the quick turnaround and the credit offer.

We'll attach two more traces if anything shows up during our next run, but everything looks stable for now. Marcus, happy to join a short bridge tomorrow at 10:30 PST if you want to walk through the technical changes.

Thanks,
Priya

--
Priya Rao
Head of ML Ops, Silverline AI

> Quick update now that our mitigations have been applied and validated across your routes:
> ...

Impact: the nightly scoring job failed to finish; downstream A/B scoring is delayed. Can Redwood confirm whether anything changed on your side? We don't have any configuration changes on our end.

I've attached a short sample trace and a timeline CSV export. Please let us know next steps and whether we should divert traffic to an alternate model variant. We're happy to schedule a quick bridge.

Thanks,
Priya Rao
Head of ML Ops
Silverline AI

Attachments: silverline-sample-trace-20261116.log, silverline-timeline-20261116.csv


From: Aisha Rahman <aisha_rahman@redwood.com>
To: Priya Rao <priya.rao@silverline.ai>, Ethan Cole <ethan.cole@silverline.ai>
Cc: Rishi Malhotra <rishi_malhotra@redwood.com>, Monica Patel <monica_patel@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 16 Nov 2026 09:12:00 -0800
Subject: Re: [ACTION REQUEST] silverline3b — intermittent 5xx/timeouts impacting batch inference

Hi Priya — thanks for the quick heads up and for the attachments. We see the same error spikes on our side and have opened a high-priority triage (INF-4521).

Immediate actions we've taken:
- Routed your production route to the silverline3b-fallback variant (same weights) to reduce exposure to the affected shard.
- Throttled concurrent batch window for requests >6k tokens to reduce peak KV pressure.
- Ops is collecting debug dumps; can you also forward any additional traces for 5xx occurrences (X-Request-Id header values are helpful).

Rishi (SRE) and Monica (CS) are on the thread — Rishi is leading the technical investigation and we'll provide a timeline update within 3 hours. Apologies for the disruption; we'll also follow up with a full post-incident summary and remediation plan.

Best,
Aisha Rahman
Account Executive | Redwood Inference

Note: Temporary routing change in place — we will not change your billed model variant without sign-off.


From: Rishi Malhotra <rishi_malhotra@redwood.com>
To: Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.com>, Monica Patel <monica_patel@redwood.com>
Cc: Aisha Rahman <aisha_rahman@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 16 Nov 2026 10:05:00 -0800
Subject: [INTERNAL] initial findings: silverline3b 5xx spike (KV cache eviction)

Marcus / Monica — quick sync notes from the triage:
- Symptom: sudden increase in 5xxs originating in the model-serving process when handling requests with context >8k tokens. Error path shows failed async disk spills from the KV cache layer followed by an unhandled exception in the request worker.
- Root cause (interim): an eviction storm on per-model KV shards caused by a corner-case in the LRU eviction where async spill tasks were delayed under IO pressure; when the spill failed the worker would surface a 5xx instead of gracefully falling back to in-memory degrade path.
- Evidence: attached kv-eviction-dump (sample of shard stats) and server traces correlate the spike with multiple parallel batch windows starting at ~08:28 PST.

Mitigations applied (10:00 PST):
1) Hotfix deployed to worker process to catch spill failures and route to the in-memory fallback (kv-spill-safety v1).
2) Increased KV in-memory threshold for silverline3b to reduce spill frequency for the next 48h.
3) Backpressure rule added: concurrent long-context batches for the route limited to 6 at once.

Next steps:
- Full patch to adjust eviction policy and improve async retry semantics (Marcus to own).
- Canary the fix into silverline3b-fallback tomorrow morning (small %).
- Postmortem to include monitoring hooks for pending async spill queue length and failed-spill-rate alarm.

I'll add timing notes to INF-4521 and attach the debug artifacts.

Rishi Malhotra
Principal SRE, Redwood Inference

Attachments: kv-eviction-dump-20261116.json.zip


From: Aisha Rahman <aisha_rahman@redwood.com>
To: Priya Rao <priya.rao@silverline.ai>, Ethan Cole <ethan.cole@silverline.ai>
Cc: Rishi Malhotra <rishi_malhotra@redwood.com>, Monica Patel <monica_patel@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 16 Nov 2026 16:42:00 -0800
Subject: silverline3b update: mitigation steps completed and next steps

Hi Priya / Ethan —

Quick update now that our mitigations have been applied and validated across your routes:

What happened (short):
We observed an eviction cascade in the per-model KV cache that, under sustained long-context batch traffic, triggered async spill failures which were not being handled by the worker. That failure path produced elevated 5xx/timeouts for requests that needed KV spill I/O.

What we did for you (completed):
- Turned on kv-spill-safety v1 which intercepts spill failures and returns the in-memory degrade path instead of failing the request; deployed at 10:00 PST.
- Increased the in-memory threshold for silverline3b for 48 hours to avoid immediate spills while we finish the fix.
- Temporarily routed your traffic to the fallback variant and added short-term concurrency limits for long-context batches.

Result: Error rate for your account fell from ~12% to <0.8% across the afternoon sampling window. You should see normal job completion for your nightly scoring run; Monica will validate job completion with your team.

Planned longer-term fixes (timeline):
- Eviction policy change + improved async spill retry semantics (engineering patch) — target: 2026-11-19 canary deploy.
- New alarms for failed-spill-rate and pending-spill-queue (in monitoring) — target: 2026-11-18.
- Postmortem and RCA doc to be shared by 2026-11-20 with concrete remediation and compensating controls.

Compensation: We will apply a credit for the impacted time window against November usage; our billing team will follow up once the window is finalized.

Action items for Silverline AI:
- If you see any remaining 5xxs, please forward the X-Request-Id values or the trace attachments to support@redwood.com so we can correlate.
- If you’d like a short bridge tomorrow for Marcus to walk through the technical fix, let me know preferred times.

Thanks for your patience — we know this disrupted an important job and we appreciate the quick signal from your team.

Best,
Aisha Rahman
Account Executive | Redwood Inference
Direct: (415) 555-0193

Aisha Rahman

source.subject: Token attribution rules for system/tool tokens — rate card confirmation
messages:
From: Mark Dalton <mark.dalton@fintechco.com>
To: Aisha Rahman <aisha_rahman@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-11-15 09:12 PST
Subject: Clarify token counting for contract rate-card & discount tiers

Hi Aisha,

Thanks for the quick onboarding last week — we're getting close to finalizing the SOW but need one final piece for procurement: a clear, written explanation of how tokens are counted for our hosted usage so we can put the math in the contract. A few concrete scenarios below — can you confirm how each is billed on the hosted API (unit counts, rounding rules, and how discounts will apply):

1) System vs. user messages: We have a persistent system prompt (200 tokens) + user prompt (120 tokens). Is the system prompt always billed as input tokens every request, or is there any prefix-caching/exemption?

2) Tool/function calls: If the assistant invokes a function with arguments we send (e.g., a 300-token JSON payload) and the tool returns a 150-token structured response, do we get charged for the function-call inputs as input tokens and the returned content as output tokens?

3) Multiple small requests vs. batching: If we send two separate requests of 10 tokens each vs. a single batched request of 20 tokens, do you do rounding per request or per-batch? Any minimum per-API-call rounding behavior?

4) Streaming & partial tokens: We stream generated tokens back to the client. Does streaming change rounding, or are tokens counted as generated tokens once emitted?

5) Discounts: Our procurement team needs to know if discounts apply per-token unit price, or on the overall invoice after usage aggregation. Also, how are committed tiers applied if we go over the commitment in a given month?

I've attached a small sample usage CSV so you can show a worked example (usage_example.csv). Procurement will need this detail for the PO.

Appreciate a short written response and if possible a one-pager/example calculation we can attach to the contract.

Best,
Mark
Procurement, FinTechCo
[Attachment: usage_example.csv (csv, 3 KB)]

From: Aisha Rahman <aisha_rahman@redwood.com>
To: Mark Dalton <mark.dalton@fintechco.com>
Cc: Monica Patel <monica_patel@redwood.com>, Laura Bennett <laura_bennett@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-11-15 11:03 PST
Subject: Re: Clarify token counting for contract rate-card & discount tiers

Hi Mark — thanks for the clear scenarios, that helps. I've attached our current hosted rate card and a quick example calc (ratecard_v3.xlsx) and have looped in Monica (billing) and Laura (pricing) who can confirm the exact rounding/discount mechanics. Quick answers up-front:

- System prompts are billed as input on every request unless you use our prefix caching feature and the prefix is a cache hit (we can include caching rules in the SOW).
- Function/tool inputs are counted as input tokens; tool/function responses are counted as output tokens.
- Rounding is done per request (we charge integer token counts per API call) — Monica will confirm exactly how batching is handled.
- Streaming does not change semantics: tokens are counted when generated and billed as output tokens.
- Discounts: typically applied as a per-unit discount on the usage line items (i.e., token unit price is discounted once thresholds/commitments are applied). Laura will add the precedence rules for committed vs. overage pricing.

Can we set a 30-minute call tomorrow to walk through the CSV and produce the one-pager? I'm free 10–11am or 3–4pm PT.

Thanks,
Aisha
Account Executive — Redwood Inference
[Attachment: ratecard_v3.xlsx (xlsx, 98 KB)]

From: Monica Patel <monica_patel@redwood.com>
To: Mark Dalton <mark.dalton@redwood.com>, Aisha Rahman <aisha_rahman@redwood.com>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura_bennett@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-11-15 15:22 PST
Subject: Re: Clarify token counting for contract rate-card & discount tiers

Hi Mark — Monica from Billing here. Below are the concrete billing rules we apply for hosted API usage (I'll reference the line items in ratecard_v3.xlsx):

1) System vs. user messages
- All message content sent to the model as part of a request (system, user, assistant prefixes) is counted as input tokens for that request.
- Prefix caching: if you enable prefix caching and the prefix is a cache hit, we do not bill the cached token portion. Cache eligibility depends on exact prefix match and the model + version. We can add cache rules to the SOW.

2) Tool/Function calls
- Tool call arguments (the JSON you send as function arguments) are billed as input tokens on the request that invokes the function.
- The tool/system response returned by the function is billed as output tokens on the same API session — treated as generated output associated with that request (billed at the output rate for the model).
- If you then include the tool response in a follow-up request, that included content is billed again as input in the follow-up. There is no automatic deduplication across separate requests.

3) Batching and rounding
- Token counting is exact (integer token counts). We measure tokens per request and round up to the nearest whole token per request — there is no sub-token billing.
- If you submit multiple logical prompts in a single API call (i.e., a batch payload where you combine text), we count tokens on the combined request; rounding is applied on that combined request. Therefore, batching small prompts into a single call can avoid per-call rounding overhead.

4) Streaming
- Streaming doesn't change billing semantics: tokens are billed as output tokens as they are generated. We do not apply special per-chunk rounding; only the final generated token count is used (again rounded to whole tokens).

5) Discounts & commitments
- Discounts are applied at the per-token unit price level according to the tiering in your contract.
- Commitments: committed monthly spend reduces the effective unit price up to the committed amount. Overage above the commitment is billed at the published overage rate (which may be higher) unless you have an overage protection clause.
- Example (see ratecard_v3.xlsx): if your base unit price is $0.0008/token and you have a 10% committed-use discount up to 1B tokens, the discounted unit price for tokens within the commitment is $0.00072. Tokens above 1B are billed at the overage price.

I've attached a worked example PDF in the ratecard workbook — happy to walk through line-by-line on a call.

Thanks,
Monica Patel
Billing Lead — Redwood Inference

From: Mark Dalton <mark.dalton@fintechco.com>
To: Monica Patel <monica_patel@redwood.com>, Aisha Rahman <aisha_rahman@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-11-16 09:10 PST
Subject: Re: Clarify token counting for contract rate-card & discount tiers

Thanks Monica — that's helpful. A couple follow-ups (sorry for the detail, procurement insists on edge-case clarity):

A) Deduplication / identical tool responses: If our architecture makes repeated identical function calls and the tool returns identical content (e.g., cached downstream microservice returns "OK"), does Redwood deduplicate identical generated outputs across requests or still bill each generate?

B) External fetches inside function args: We sometimes pass a short pointer to a large payload that the function runtime fetches externally (the argu- ment is just a URL + metadata, small token footprint). The fetched content is not included in the original request tokenization — do you bill the fetched content if it is materialized into the function response?

C) Sample numbers: Could you show a quick worked example for the following: system prompt 200 tokens + user 120 tokens + function args 300 tokens triggering a 150-token function response + final assistant reply 80 tokens. Show token counts (input vs output), rounding, and unit price math for the first 10,000 requests with a 10% commitment discount up to 1M tokens.

If you can post that in the thread (or in a short PDF), procurement can finish the PO. Also we prefer a single page that we can attach to the SOW explaining rounding and discount application.

Thanks,
Mark

From: Laura Bennett <laura_bennett@redwood.com>
To: Mark Dalton <mark.dalton@redwood.com>, Aisha Rahman <aisha_rahman@redwood.com>
Cc: Monica Patel <monica_patel@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-11-16 12:30 PST
Subject: Re: Clarify token counting for contract rate-card & discount tiers

Hi Mark — Laura from Pricing. Short answers on commercial mechanics, and I'll attach a scenario calc to the HubSpot deal (link below).

- Deduplication: We do not deduplicate across distinct API calls. Each request that results in generated output is billed for that generated content. If you rely on cache hits at the prefix level (see Monica's note), that may avoid billing for the cached portion, but identical outputs from separate calls are billed separately.

- External fetches: If the function runtime fetches external payloads and returns content in the function response that is then emitted by the model, that generated content is billed as output tokens. The fact that the original request had a URL pointer does not change that — the billing is based on what was sent to/from the model at tokenization time and what the model generated.

- Requested worked example (summary):
  * Per-request tokenization:
    - Input tokens: system (200) + user (120) + function args (300) = 620 input tokens billed on the invoking request.
    - Output tokens: function response (150) + final assistant reply (80) = 230 output tokens billed (these are generated output associated with the session).
  * Per-request rounding: tokens are whole integers; we don't inflate beyond the actual token counts except rounding up to the nearest integer (which in practice is a no-op since token counts are integers).
  * Unit price math (example): base input unit price = $0.0004/token; base output unit price = $0.0008/token. With a 10% commitment discount up to 1,000,000 tokens, discounted input price = $0.00036; discounted output price = $0.00072 for tokens within commitment.

  Example calc for 10,000 identical requests:
    - Total input tokens = 620 * 10,000 = 6,200,000 tokens
    - Total output tokens = 230 * 10,000 = 2,300,000 tokens
    - Tokens covered by 1,000,000-token commitment (applies to whichever line is specified in your contract — typically total tokens combined or broken out by type; we can make it per total tokens if you prefer): assuming commitment applies to combined tokens and is consumed first, 1,000,000 tokens get discounted. The remaining tokens billed at overage rates.

I'll drop a short PDF to the HubSpot deal and to this thread with the line-by-line numbers; Laura (pricing) note: exact precedence (whether commitment consumes input vs output first) is negotiable and should be specified in the contract; defaults are in the attached ratecard.

Link: https://app.hubspot.com/deals/1234567 (worked example uploaded)

Cheers,
Laura Bennett
Pricing Manager — Redwood Inference

From: Aisha Rahman <aisha_rahman@redwood.com>
To: Mark Dalton <mark.dalton@fintechco.com>
Cc: Monica Patel <monica_patel@redwood.com>, Laura Bennett <laura_bennett@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-11-17 08:45 PST
Subject: Re: Clarify token counting for contract rate-card & discount tiers

Thanks Mark — looping back with the attachments and next steps.

Attached: SOW_FinTechCo_v1.pdf (draft SOW with an appendix covering prefix caching) and the ratecard workbook (ratecard_v3.xlsx) with the worked examples Laura referenced.

Next steps:
- I'll schedule a 30-minute call Wednesday 11/18 at 10am PT to review the one-pager line-by-line and produce the signed example for procurement.
- Laura/Monica: please prepare the single-page worked example (explicit numbers & assumption about whether commitment consumes total tokens or per-line) and upload it to the HubSpot deal.
- Mark / procurement: if you want commitment accounting to be per-line (input vs output) instead of a combined token pool, please confirm prior to the call so we can show both options.

If this looks good I'll send calendar invites and a DocuSign link for the SOW after we agree the appendix language.

Best,
Aisha Rahman
Account Executive — Redwood Inference
[Attachment: SOW_FinTechCo_v1.pdf (pdf, 42 KB)]

Next steps:

- Laura/Monica: please prepare the single-page worked example (explicit numbers & assumption about whether commitment consumes total tokens or per-line) and upload it to the HubSpot deal.
- Mark / procurement: if you want commitment accounting to be per-line (input vs output) instead of a combined token pool, please confirm prior to the call so we can show both options.

If this looks good I'll send calendar invites and a DocuSign link for the SOW after we agree the appendix language.

text:
From: Mark Dalton <mark.dalton@fintechco.com>
To: Aisha Rahman <aisha_rahman@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-11-15 09:12 PST
Subject: Clarify token counting for contract rate-card & discount tiers

Hi Aisha,

Thanks for the quick onboarding last week — we're getting close to finalizing the SOW but need one final piece for procurement: a clear, written explanation of how tokens are counted for our hosted usage so we can put the math in the contract. A few concrete scenarios below — can you confirm how each is billed on the hosted API (unit counts, rounding rules, and how discounts will apply):

1) System vs. user messages: We have a persistent system prompt (200 tokens) + user prompt (120 tokens). Is the system prompt always billed as input tokens every request, or is there any prefix-caching/exemption?

2) Tool/function calls: If the assistant invokes a function with arguments we send (e.g., a 300-token JSON payload) and the tool returns a 150-token structured response, do we get charged for the function-call inputs as input tokens and the returned content as output tokens?

3) Multiple small requests vs. batching: If we send two separate requests of 10 tokens each vs. a single batched request of 20 tokens, do you do rounding per request or per-batch? Any minimum per-API-call rounding behavior?

4) Streaming & partial tokens: We stream generated tokens back to the client. Does streaming change rounding, or are tokens counted as generated tokens once emitted?

5) Discounts: Our procurement team needs to know if discounts apply per-token unit price, or on the overall invoice after usage aggregation. Also, how are committed tiers applied if we go over the commitment in a given month?

I've attached a small sample usage CSV so you can show a worked example (usage_example.csv). Procurement will need this detail for the PO.

Appreciate a short written response and if possible a one-pager/example calculation we can attach to the contract.

Best,
Mark
Procurement, FinTechCo
[Attachment: usage_example.csv (csv, 3 KB)]

From: Aisha Rahman <aisha_rahman@redwood.com>
To: Mark Dalton <mark.dalton@fintechco.com>
Cc: Monica Patel <monica_patel@redwood.com>, Laura Bennett <laura_bennett@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-11-15 11:03 PST
Subject: Re: Clarify token counting for contract rate-card & discount tiers

Hi Mark — thanks for the clear scenarios, that helps. I've attached our current hosted rate card and a quick example calc (ratecard_v3.xlsx) and have looped in Monica (billing) and Laura (pricing) who can confirm the exact rounding/discount mechanics. Quick answers up-front:

- System prompts are billed as input on every request unless you use our prefix caching feature and the prefix is a cache hit (we can include caching rules in the SOW).
- Function/tool inputs are counted as input tokens; tool/function responses are counted as output tokens.
- Rounding is done per request (we charge integer token counts per API call) — Monica will confirm exactly how batching is handled.
- Streaming does not change semantics: tokens are counted when generated and billed as output tokens.
- Discounts: typically applied as a per-unit discount on the usage line items (i.e., token unit price is discounted once thresholds/commitments are applied). Laura will add the precedence rules for committed vs. overage pricing.

Can we set a 30-minute call tomorrow to walk through the CSV and produce the one-pager? I'm free 10–11am or 3–4pm PT.

Thanks,
Aisha
Account Executive — Redwood Inference
[Attachment: ratecard_v3.xlsx (xlsx, 98 KB)]

From: Monica Patel <monica_patel@redwood.com>
To: Mark Dalton <mark.dalton@redwood.com>, Aisha Rahman <aisha_rahman@redwood.com>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura_bennett@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-11-15 15:22 PST
Subject: Re: Clarify token counting for contract rate-card & discount tiers

Hi Mark — Monica from Billing here. Below are the concrete billing rules we apply for hosted API usage (I'll reference the line items in ratecard_v3.xlsx):

1) System vs. user messages
- All message content sent to the model as part of a request (system, user, assistant prefixes) is counted as input tokens for that request.
- Prefix caching: if you enable prefix caching and the prefix is a cache hit, we do not bill the cached token portion. Cache eligibility depends on exact prefix match and the model + version. We can add cache rules to the SOW.

2) Tool/Function calls
- Tool call arguments (the JSON you send as function arguments) are billed as input tokens on the request that invokes the function.
- The tool/system response returned by the function is billed as output tokens on the same API session — treated as generated output associated with that request (billed at the output rate for the model).
- If you then include the tool response in a follow-up request, that included content is billed again as input in the follow-up. There is no automatic deduplication across separate requests.

3) Batching and rounding
- Token counting is exact (integer token counts). We measure tokens per request and round up to the nearest whole token per request — there is no sub-token billing.
- If you submit multiple logical prompts in a single API call (i.e., a batch payload where you combine text), we count tokens on the combined request; rounding is applied on that combined request. Therefore, batching small prompts into a single call can avoid per-call rounding overhead.

4) Streaming
- Streaming doesn't change billing semantics: tokens are billed as output tokens as they are generated. We do not apply special per-chunk rounding; only the final generated token count is used (again rounded to whole tokens).

5) Discounts & commitments
- Discounts are applied at the per-token unit price level according to the tiering in your contract.
- Commitments: committed monthly spend reduces the effective unit price up to the committed amount. Overage above the commitment is billed at the published overage rate (which may be higher) unless you have an overage protection clause.
- Example (see ratecard_v3.xlsx): if your base unit price is $0.0008/token and you have a 10% committed-use discount up to 1B tokens, the discounted unit price for tokens within the commitment is $0.00072. Tokens above 1B are billed at the overage price.

I've attached a worked example PDF in the ratecard workbook — happy to walk through line-by-line on a call.

Thanks,
Monica Patel
Billing Lead — Redwood Inference


From: Mark Dalton <mark.dalton@fintechco.com>
To: Monica Patel <monica_patel@redwood.com>, Aisha Rahman <aisha_rahman@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-11-16 09:10 PST
Subject: Re: Clarify token counting for contract rate-card & discount tiers

Thanks Monica — that's helpful. A couple follow-ups (sorry for the detail, procurement insists on edge-case clarity):

A) Deduplication / identical tool responses: If our architecture makes repeated identical function calls and the tool returns identical content (e.g., cached downstream microservice returns "OK"), does Redwood deduplicate identical generated outputs across requests or still bill each generate?

B) External fetches inside function args: We sometimes pass a short pointer to a large payload that the function runtime fetches externally (the argu- ment is just a URL + metadata, small token footprint). The fetched content is not included in the original request tokenization — do you bill the fetched content if it is materialized into the function response?

C) Sample numbers: Could you show a quick worked example for the following: system prompt 200 tokens + user 120 tokens + function args 300 tokens triggering a 150-token function response + final assistant reply 80 tokens. Show token counts (input vs output), rounding, and unit price math for the first 10,000 requests with a 10% commitment discount up to 1M tokens.

If you can post that in the thread (or in a short PDF), procurement can finish the PO. Also we prefer a single page that we can attach to the SOW explaining rounding and discount application.

Thanks,
Mark


From: Laura Bennett <laura_bennett@redwood.com>
To: Mark Dalton <mark.dalton@redwood.com>, Aisha Rahman <aisha_rahman@redwood.com>
Cc: Monica Patel <monica_patel@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-11-16 12:30 PST
Subject: Re: Clarify token counting for contract rate-card & discount tiers

Hi Mark — Laura from Pricing. Short answers on commercial mechanics, and I'll attach a scenario calc to the HubSpot deal (link below).

- Deduplication: We do not deduplicate across distinct API calls. Each request that results in generated output is billed for that generated content. If you rely on cache hits at the prefix level (see Monica's note), that may avoid billing for the cached portion, but identical outputs from separate calls are billed separately.

- External fetches: If the function runtime fetches external payloads and returns content in the function response that is then emitted by the model, that generated content is billed as output tokens. The fact that the original request had a URL pointer does not change that — the billing is based on what was sent to/from the model at tokenization time and what the model generated.

- Requested worked example (summary):
  * Per-request tokenization:
    - Input tokens: system (200) + user (120) + function args (300) = 620 input tokens billed on the invoking request.
    - Output tokens: function response (150) + final assistant reply (80) = 230 output tokens billed (these are generated output associated with the session).
  * Per-request rounding: tokens are whole integers; we don't inflate beyond the actual token counts except rounding up to the nearest integer (which in practice is a no-op since token counts are integers).
  * Unit price math (example): base input unit price = $0.0004/token; base output unit price = $0.0008/token. With a 10% commitment discount up to 1,000,000 tokens, discounted input price = $0.00036; discounted output price = $0.00072 for tokens within commitment.

  Example calc for 10,000 identical requests:
    - Total input tokens = 620 * 10,000 = 6,200,000 tokens
    - Total output tokens = 230 * 10,000 = 2,300,000 tokens
    - Tokens covered by 1,000,000-token commitment (applies to whichever line is specified in your contract — typically total tokens combined or broken out by type; we can make it per total tokens if you prefer): assuming commitment applies to combined tokens and is consumed first, 1,000,000 tokens get discounted. The remaining tokens billed at overage rates.

I'll drop a short PDF to the HubSpot deal and to this thread with the line-by-line numbers; Laura (pricing) note: exact precedence (whether commitment consumes input vs output first) is negotiable and should be specified in the contract; defaults are in the attached ratecard.

Link: https://app.hubspot.com/deals/1234567 (worked example uploaded)

Cheers,
Laura Bennett
Pricing Manager — Redwood Inference


From: Aisha Rahman <aisha_rahman@redwood.com>
To: Mark Dalton <mark.dalton@fintechco.com>
Cc: Monica Patel <monica_patel@redwood.com>, Laura Bennett <laura_bennett@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-11-17 08:45 PST
Subject: Re: Clarify token counting for contract rate-card & discount tiers

Thanks Mark — looping back with the attachments and next steps.

Attached: SOW_FinTechCo_v1.pdf (draft SOW with an appendix covering prefix caching) and the ratecard workbook (ratecard_v3.xlsx) with the worked examples Laura referenced.

Next steps:

- Laura/Monica: please prepare the single-page worked example (explicit numbers & assumption about whether commitment consumes total tokens or per-line) and upload it to the HubSpot deal.
- Mark / procurement: if you want commitment accounting to be per-line (input vs output) instead of a combined token pool, please confirm prior to the call so we can show both options.

If this looks good I'll send calendar invites and a DocuSign link for the SOW after we agree the appendix language.

Best,
Aisha Rahman

To: Laura Chen <laura.chen@deltaedge.ai>

Date: Tue, 10 Nov 2026 09:12:00 -0800
Subject: Hosted API pricing ask + commitment (DeltaEdge)

Laura —

Thanks again for the time earlier. Per our conversation, DeltaEdge is prepared to commit to a 12-month, US-only Hosted API contract with an initial monthly commit of 120M generation tokens (expected ramp to 200M/mo by month 6) in exchange for predictable unit pricing. They are requesting a 25% unit-rate reduction vs our standard published hosted rates for that commit window.

Key points:
- Account: DeltaEdge (series B, vertical: supply-chain analytics)
- Initial commit: 120M generation tokens / month; expected to scale to ~200M/mo within 6 months
- Contract length: 12 months; renewal optional with review at month 10
- Requested concession: 25% off standard Hosted API per-token rate for committed volume
- Other asks: SOC2 docs, VPC peering for traffic ingress, and a one-page mutual action plan

I attached the updated MMP and our working pricing model for transparency. This ask exceeds the typical deal-desk threshold (>20%), so we need executive approval for an exception. DeltaEdge has indicated timeline pressure — they aim to sign before Thanksgiving.

Why I think we should consider it:
- Net-new strategic account in manufacturing/supply chain with strong usage profile; good reference value in North America
- Predictable committed volume with rapid ramp reduces short-term volatility in hosted capacity planning
- We can structure guardrails: 12-month term, commit true-ups, and an early-renewal cap tied to consumption tiers

Requested action: Exec authorization for up to 25% concession on committed Hosted API pricing for 12 months, with the above guardrails. If approved, I will finalize the SOW and coordinate security and legal prerequisites.

Thanks — happy to jump on a quick call if helpful.

Best,
Ben
Ben Carter
Senior Account Executive, Strategic Accounts
Redwood Inference

From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
To: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>, Mina Haddad <mina.haddad@redwoodinference.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Thu, 12 Nov 2026 08:21:00 -0800
Subject: Internal: DeltaEdge exec approval ask (25% requested) — numbers + recommendation

Team — quick internal note and ask for signoff path. DeltaEdge is pushing for a decision by 24 Nov. Below are the economics and my recommendation.

Simple economics (monthly committed band):
- Published hosted unit rate (baseline): $0.0125 per 1k gen tokens
- 25% off -> Effective rate: $0.009375 per 1k gen tokens
- Initial commit: 120,000k tokens -> monthly invoice at effective rate = $1,125.00
- Expected ramp to 200,000k tokens -> monthly invoice = $1,875.00
- Annualized committed revenue (year 1, conservative ramp schedule): ~$18k – this is modest ARR but high usage velocity and visibility into production workload

Upside / mitigations:
- Reference potential with 2 pilot customers in their enterprise cohort; good marketing + cross-sell signal
- Guardrails: 12-month term, commit true-up, auto-suspend thresholds beyond gross overage without executive clearance
- Option: include a limited add-on for a cost-recovery integration fee for VPC peering and expedited SOC2 evidence prep

Risk: unit economics on token-heavy workloads are material; >20% is outside typical delegated authority. However, given the strategic value and the short fixed term, I recommend we approve a 25% concession for 12 months conditioned on legal-approved SOW and procurement milestones.

Requested: Marcus — can you provide executive signoff or a path to escalate to the C-suite? Mina — please confirm finance comfort with the guardrails and whether we need an alternate billing floor.

Thanks,
Ben

> On Wed, Nov 11, 2026 at 10:03 AM Laura Chen <laura.chen@deltaedge.ai> wrote:
> > Ben —
> > 
> > Appreciate the clear summary. Samir and I discussed this internally — we can move forward quickly if we get confirmation of the 25% positioned as a pilot-year concession ...
From: Mina Haddad <mina.haddad@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Fri, 13 Nov 2026 17:46:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Internal: DeltaEdge exec approval ask (25% requested) — numbers + recommendation

Ben / team —

Finance reviewed the model and the proposed guardrails. Key notes:
- With commit true-ups and a 12-month term, the downside is limited. We recommend a $15k monthly minimum billing floor for the first 3 months to ensure coverage of onboarding and SOC2 evidence prep costs (this mimics a cost-recovery approach).
- Approve up to 25% concession for 12 months on the Hosted API committed band contingent on:
  1) Legal-approved SOW that includes a consumption true-up clause,
  2) $15k monthly minimum for months 1–3,
  3) Standard clause: we reserve the right to revert to published rates on renewal with 60 days notice.

Marcus — please confirm executive signoff (I recommend we proceed with Ben to finalize paperwork and set the DocuSign template live once Marcus signs off). If you endorse, I will coordinate finance accounting entries for the unusual concession.

Thanks,
Mina Haddad
Head of Finance – Revenue Operations
Redwood Inference

--

DeltaEdge

On Tue, Nov 10, 2026 at 9:12 AM Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com> wrote:
> Laura —
> 
> Thanks again for the time earlier. Per our conversation, DeltaEdge is prepared to commit to a 12-month, US-only Hosted API contract with an initial monthly commit of 120M generation tokens ...


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
To: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>, Mina Haddad <mina.haddad@redwoodinference.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Thu, 12 Nov 2026 08:21:00 -0800
Subject: Internal: DeltaEdge exec approval ask (25% requested) — numbers + recommendation

Team — quick internal note and ask for signoff path. DeltaEdge is pushing for a decision by 24 Nov. Below are the economics and my recommendation.

Simple economics (monthly committed band):
- Published hosted unit rate (baseline): $0.0125 per 1k gen tokens
- 25% off -> Effective rate: $0.009375 per 1k gen tokens
- Initial commit: 120,000k tokens -> monthly invoice at effective rate = $1,125.00
- Expected ramp to 200,000k tokens -> monthly invoice = $1,875.00
- Annualized committed revenue (year 1, conservative ramp schedule): ~$18k – this is modest ARR but high usage velocity and visibility into production workload

Upside / mitigations:
- Reference potential with 2 pilot customers in their enterprise cohort; good marketing + cross-sell signal
- Guardrails: 12-month term, commit true-up, auto-suspend thresholds beyond gross overage without executive clearance
- Option: include a limited add-on for a cost-recovery integration fee for VPC peering and expedited SOC2 evidence prep

Risk: unit economics on token-heavy workloads are material; >20% is outside typical delegated authority. However, given the strategic value and the short fixed term, I recommend we approve a 25% concession for 12 months conditioned on legal-approved SOW and procurement milestones.

Requested: Marcus — can you provide executive signoff or a path to escalate to the C-suite? Mina — please confirm finance comfort with the guardrails and whether we need an alternate billing floor.

Thanks,
Ben

> On Wed, Nov 11, 2026 at 10:03 AM Laura Chen <laura.chen@deltaedge.ai> wrote:
> > Ben —
> > 
> > Appreciate the clear summary. Samir and I discussed this internally — we can move forward quickly if we get confirmation of the 25% positioned as a pilot-year concession ...


From: Mina Haddad <mina.haddad@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Fri, 13 Nov 2026 17:46:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Internal: DeltaEdge exec approval ask (25% requested) — numbers + recommendation

Ben / team —

Finance reviewed the model and the proposed guardrails. Key notes:
- With commit true-ups and a 12-month term, the downside is limited. We recommend a $15k monthly minimum billing floor for the first 3 months to ensure coverage of onboarding and SOC2 evidence prep costs (this mimics a cost-recovery approach).
- Approve up to 25% concession for 12 months on the Hosted API committed band contingent on:
  1) Legal-approved SOW that includes a consumption true-up clause,
  2) $15k monthly minimum for months 1–3,
  3) Standard clause: we reserve the right to revert to published rates on renewal with 60 days notice.

Marcus — please confirm executive signoff (I recommend we proceed with Ben to finalize paperwork and set the DocuSign template live once Marcus signs off). If you endorse, I will coordinate finance accounting entries for the unusual concession.

Thanks,
Mina Haddad
Head of Finance – Revenue Operations
Redwood Inference

source.subject: Customer key escrow exception request for private edge handoff
messages:
From: Sam Torres <sam.torres@medisafe.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>, Legal Intake <legal@medisafe.com>
Date: Mon, 9 Nov 2026 14:12:00 -0800
Subject: Request: Exception to key escrow for on-premedge deployment

Hi Ava / Legal team,

Quick note following our technical workshop last week — Medisafe is moving forward with a private-edge deployment that will sit in our hospital partner data centers. The deployment model requires that the customer holds the master key (BYOK) and that Redwood has no ability to decrypt production data. Our security team understands Redwood's standard escrow/escrow-operator model, however we need an exception to the keystore escrow clause in the DPA for this specific deployment.

Main asks:
- Can Redwood confirm a formal exception path to avoid key escrow for on-prem / customer-controlled KMS?
- What operational controls would replace escrow to give us assurance (audit logs, operator access windows, breakglass auditability)?
- Any subprocessors that would need access to the deployed environment and their controls?

We'd like a written summary we can share with our compliance board by Friday. I've copied Priya (legal) and Legal Intake for intake.

Thanks,
Sam
Sam Torres
Sr. Security Program Manager, Medisafe Health

Attachment: Medisafe_DPA_Annex_A.pdf (attachment stub)
From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>
To: Sam Torres <sam.torres@medisafe.com>
Cc: Dev Patel <dev.patel@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 9 Nov 2026 16:01:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Request: Exception to key escrow for on-premedge deployment

Sam —

Thanks for the clear summary. We're supportive of a model where the customer retains the master CMK in their HSM and Redwood operates against a wrapped/delegated key in the private deployment. To address your specific asks at a high level:

1) Exception path:
- We can include a DPA addendum that permits an escrow exception for deployments that meet predefined technical gates (isolated VPC, no cross-tenant networking, customer-managed KMS with attested HSM).
- The addendum would include explicit operator access constraints (time-boxed maintenance windows, jump-host logging, and MFA-brokered sessions).

2) Operational controls proposed to replace escrow:
- Continuous operator action logging shipped to a customer-owned SIEM (Syslog/CloudWatch endpoint) and immutable audit archives retained for 1 year.
- Breakglass process: emergency access only via OPA-reviewed request with two-person approval and egress captured to an independent audit bucket.
- Proof-of-control evidence: weekly tamper-evident snapshots and signed attestations for the first 90 days post-handoff.

3) Subprocessors & access:
- For on-prem deployments we generally limit subprocessors: our telemetry/observability vendor will process anonymized telemetry; our managed-ops partner (if engaged) signs an OTO addendum restricting key access.
- We'll provide a subprocessor list and per-subprocessor control matrix in the SOW appendix.

Next steps I'd propose: Redwood drafts the DPA addendum + SOW appendix and a 1‑page operational controls summary for your compliance board. Dev can sketch the technical gating criteria and Marcus will produce an operator access runbook excerpt.

I'll start the draft today — copying Dev and Marcus to capture operational details. Is Friday acceptable for the compliance summary?

Best,
Ava Chen
Senior Solutions Engineer, Redwood Inference

Attachment: SOW-custkey-exception-draft.docx (attachment stub)
From: Dev Patel <dev.patel@redwood.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>, Sam Torres <sam.torres@medisafe.com>
Cc: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>, Evelyn Hart <evelyn.hart@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 10 Nov 2026 09:22:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Request: Exception to key escrow for on-premedge deployment

Folks —

Quick technical notes to feed into the draft:
- Gate criteria for escrow exception (minimum): isolated VPC with explicit ACLs, no egress to Redwood-controlled networks except our logging endpoint, customer CMK in FIPS-140 cert HSM with attestation chain, and operator sessions constrained to bastion with recorded session logs.
- Cryptography flow: Redwood will use a wrapped key (per-tenant ephemeral DEK) generated at deploy time; the master CMK never leaves customer HSM. Our runtime will call a local KMS proxy; any operator actions that would normally request CMK unwrap must be performed by the customer or via a customer-approved breakglass where the unwrap audit is recorded.
- Telemetry: we will only collect anonymized service metrics and explicit operational logs; PII/payloads will not leave the customer environment.

I'll attach a short ops excerpt in the Drive link for the SOW appendix. Marcus — please confirm the runbook excerpt (operator checklist + maintenance window template) and Evelyn, can Compliance/Legal coordinate the DPA addendum wording?

-Dev

--Forwarded message--
From: Dev Patel <dev.patel@redwood.com>
To: internal-ops@redwood.com
Date: Tue, 10 Nov 2026 09:22:05 -0800
Subject: Fwd: Request: Exception to key escrow for on-premedge deployment

(Ops excerpt in Drive link)
From: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>
To: Priya Rao <priya.rao@medisafe.com>, Legal Intake <legal@medisafe.com>
Cc: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>, Dev Patel <dev.patel@redwood.com>, Evelyn Hart <evelyn.hart@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 11 Nov 2026 09:03:00 -0800
Subject: RE: Request: Exception to key escrow for on-premedge deployment

Priya / Medisafe Legal —

Thanks for the patience while we pulled the details together. We've drafted a focused DPA addendum and an SOW appendix that enumerate: escrow exception criteria, operator access controls, required attestations, and the subprocessor matrix for this deployment. Highlights:
- No escrow: master CMK remains in customer HSM; Redwood will not hold or escrow the key.
- Operator actions that may require key unwrap are limited to customer-approved maintenance windows and require 2‑party approval; all actions produce signed audit entries.
- Subprocessors: ObservabilityCo (anonymized telemetry only) and ManagedOpsPartner (no key access; signed operational constraints).
- Evidence: we can provide weekly signed audit bundles and a one-time attestation on handoff.

I've attached the DPA addendum draft link in the HubSpot/Drive items and a proposed timeline for signatures. If the board needs an executive summary, Ava — feel free to use the one-pager I drafted in the SOW link.

Next: Priya, can you review the legal language and mark any redlines. If we get alignment today we can lock the SOW and route contracts to signature and schedule the initial handoff runbook session.

Regards,
Marcus Lin
Head of Privacy & Security Ops, Redwood Inference

Attachment: DPA_Addendum_Draft.pdf (Drive link in related_links)

To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>, Legal Intake <legal@medisafe.com>
Date: Mon, 9 Nov 2026 14:12:00 -0800
Subject: Request: Exception to key escrow for on-premedge deployment

Hi Ava / Legal team,

Quick note following our technical workshop last week — Medisafe is moving forward with a private-edge deployment that will sit in our hospital partner data centers. The deployment model requires that the customer holds the master key (BYOK) and that Redwood has no ability to decrypt production data. Our security team understands Redwood's standard escrow/escrow-operator model, however we need an exception to the keystore escrow clause in the DPA for this specific deployment.

Main asks:
- Can Redwood confirm a formal exception path to avoid key escrow for on-prem / customer-controlled KMS?
- What operational controls would replace escrow to give us assurance (audit logs, operator access windows, breakglass auditability)?
- Any subprocessors that would need access to the deployed environment and their controls?

We'd like a written summary we can share with our compliance board by Friday. I've copied Priya (legal) and Legal Intake for intake.

Thanks,
Sam
Sam Torres
Sr. Security Program Manager, Medisafe Health

Attachment: Medisafe_DPA_Annex_A.pdf (attachment stub)


From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>
To: Sam Torres <sam.torres@medisafe.com>
Cc: Dev Patel <dev.patel@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 9 Nov 2026 16:01:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Request: Exception to key escrow for on-premedge deployment

Sam —

Thanks for the clear summary. We're supportive of a model where the customer retains the master CMK in their HSM and Redwood operates against a wrapped/delegated key in the private deployment. To address your specific asks at a high level:

1) Exception path:
- We can include a DPA addendum that permits an escrow exception for deployments that meet predefined technical gates (isolated VPC, no cross-tenant networking, customer-managed KMS with attested HSM).
- The addendum would include explicit operator access constraints (time-boxed maintenance windows, jump-host logging, and MFA-brokered sessions).

2) Operational controls proposed to replace escrow:
- Continuous operator action logging shipped to a customer-owned SIEM (Syslog/CloudWatch endpoint) and immutable audit archives retained for 1 year.
- Breakglass process: emergency access only via OPA-reviewed request with two-person approval and egress captured to an independent audit bucket.
- Proof-of-control evidence: weekly tamper-evident snapshots and signed attestations for the first 90 days post-handoff.

3) Subprocessors & access:
- For on-prem deployments we generally limit subprocessors: our telemetry/observability vendor will process anonymized telemetry; our managed-ops partner (if engaged) signs an OTO addendum restricting key access.
- We'll provide a subprocessor list and per-subprocessor control matrix in the SOW appendix.

Next steps I'd propose: Redwood drafts the DPA addendum + SOW appendix and a 1‑page operational controls summary for your compliance board. Dev can sketch the technical gating criteria and Marcus will produce an operator access runbook excerpt.

I'll start the draft today — copying Dev and Marcus to capture operational details. Is Friday acceptable for the compliance summary?

Best,
Ava Chen
Senior Solutions Engineer, Redwood Inference

Attachment: SOW-custkey-exception-draft.docx (attachment stub)


From: Dev Patel <dev.patel@redwood.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>, Sam Torres <sam.torres@medisafe.com>
Cc: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>, Evelyn Hart <evelyn.hart@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 10 Nov 2026 09:22:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Request: Exception to key escrow for on-premedge deployment

Folks —

Quick technical notes to feed into the draft:
- Gate criteria for escrow exception (minimum): isolated VPC with explicit ACLs, no egress to Redwood-controlled networks except our logging endpoint, customer CMK in FIPS-140 cert HSM with attestation chain, and operator sessions constrained to bastion with recorded session logs.
- Cryptography flow: Redwood will use a wrapped key (per-tenant ephemeral DEK) generated at deploy time; the master CMK never leaves customer HSM. Our runtime will call a local KMS proxy; any operator actions that would normally request CMK unwrap must be performed by the customer or via a customer-approved breakglass where the unwrap audit is recorded.
- Telemetry: we will only collect anonymized service metrics and explicit operational logs; PII/payloads will not leave the customer environment.

I'll attach a short ops excerpt in the Drive link for the SOW appendix. Marcus — please confirm the runbook excerpt (operator checklist + maintenance window template) and Evelyn, can Compliance/Legal coordinate the DPA addendum wording?

-Dev

--Forwarded message--
From: Dev Patel <dev.patel@redwood.com>
To: internal-ops@redwood.com
Date: Tue, 10 Nov 2026 09:22:05 -0800
Subject: Fwd: Request: Exception to key escrow for on-premedge deployment

(Ops excerpt in Drive link)


From: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>
To: Priya Rao <priya.rao@medisafe.com>, Legal Intake <legal@medisafe.com>
Cc: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>, Dev Patel <dev.patel@redwood.com>, Evelyn Hart <evelyn.hart@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 11 Nov 2026 09:03:00 -0800
Subject: RE: Request: Exception to key escrow for on-premedge deployment

Priya / Medisafe Legal —

Thanks for the patience while we pulled the details together. We've drafted a focused DPA addendum and an SOW appendix that enumerate: escrow exception criteria, operator access controls, required attestations, and the subprocessor matrix for this deployment. Highlights:
- No escrow: master CMK remains in customer HSM; Redwood will not hold or escrow the key.
- Operator actions that may require key unwrap are limited to customer-approved maintenance windows and require 2‑party approval; all actions produce signed audit entries.
- Subprocessors: ObservabilityCo (anonymized telemetry only) and ManagedOpsPartner (no key access; signed operational constraints).
- Evidence: we can provide weekly signed audit bundles and a one-time attestation on handoff.

I've attached the DPA addendum draft link in the HubSpot/Drive items and a proposed timeline for signatures. If the board needs an executive summary, Ava — feel free to use the one-pager I drafted in the SOW link.

Next: Priya, can you review the legal language and mark any redlines. If we get alignment today we can lock the SOW and route contracts to signature and schedule the initial handoff runbook session.

Regards,
Marcus Lin
Head of Privacy & Security Ops, Redwood Inference

Attachment: DPA_Addendum_Draft.pdf (Drive link in related_links)
